introduction: Pipes and Cistern problems are a specialized version of time-and-work, where inlet pipes fill a tank and outlet pipes (or leaks) drain it. Instead of workers, you deal with flow rates, and instead of finishing a job, you deal with filling or emptying a tank. The same rate-based thinking from time and work applies directly here.
explanation:
CONCEPT EXPLANATION

Treat the full tank as 1 unit of work. If a pipe can fill the tank in x hours, its filling rate is 1/x of the tank per hour. If a pipe can empty a full tank in y hours, its emptying rate is 1/y of the tank per hour, and it is treated as a negative contribution when combined with inlet pipes.

When multiple pipes operate together, the net rate is the sum of all inlet rates minus the sum of all outlet rates. The time to fill (or empty) the tank is the reciprocal of this net rate. If the net rate comes out negative, the tank is actually being emptied overall, not filled.

A common variant: a tank has an inlet pipe and a leak. If the pipe alone fills in x hours, but because of the leak the tank actually fills in z hours (z > x), the leak's emptying time y can be found from 1/x − 1/y = 1/z, i.e., 1/y = 1/x − 1/z.

Another variant: pipes are opened for some time and then closed, or one pipe is opened after another closes — such problems require calculating the fraction of the tank filled in each stage and adding them up.

REAL-LIFE APPLICATIONS

Water supply management, irrigation planning, industrial tank filling/draining systems, and any scenario with combined inflow and outflow rates use this exact model.

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Adding an outlet pipe's rate instead of subtracting it.
2) Adding times directly instead of adding rates (rates add, not times).
3) Forgetting to convert 'part filled in t hours' correctly when pipes run for unequal durations.
4) Missing the sign check — a negative net rate means the tank empties, not fills.
5) Not accounting for a pipe being closed partway through the problem.

IMPORTANT NOTES

• Rate = 1/time; always work with rates, never raw times, when combining pipes.

• A leak's effect is calculated as the difference between the pipe's rate alone and the actual combined rate.

• If a tank fills in x hours with pipe A alone and y hours with pipe A and B together (B being a leak), B alone would empty it in xy/(y−x) hours.

• For multi-stage problems, calculate the fraction of tank filled in each time segment separately, then sum.

formulas.title: Fill rate of a pipe
formulas.content: If a pipe fills the tank in x hours, its rate = 1/x tank per hour

formulas.title: Empty rate of a pipe
formulas.content: If a pipe empties the tank in y hours, its rate = −1/y tank per hour (subtract when combining)

formulas.title: Net combined rate
formulas.content: Net rate = Σ(inlet rates) − Σ(outlet rates); Time to fill = 1 / Net rate

formulas.title: Two pipes together
formulas.content: Together time = xy / (x + y) for two inlet pipes with individual times x and y

formulas.title: Leak calculation
formulas.content: If pipe alone fills in x hours but combined with leak takes z hours, leak alone empties in xz/(z−x) hours

shortcutTips.title: Always convert to rates
shortcutTips.tip: Never combine pipe times directly by addition — convert every pipe's time to a rate (1/time) before adding or subtracting.

shortcutTips.title: Treat leaks as negative rates
shortcutTips.tip: An outlet pipe or leak always subtracts from the net filling rate — write its contribution with a minus sign.

shortcutTips.title: Use the product-over-sum shortcut
shortcutTips.tip: For two inlet pipes with times x and y, together time = xy/(x+y), same as the two-worker time-and-work shortcut.

shortcutTips.title: Check the sign of net rate
shortcutTips.tip: If the net rate is negative, the tank is emptying overall — report the correct interpretation (time to empty, not fill).

shortcutTips.title: Use LCM for tank capacity
shortcutTips.tip: Assign the tank capacity as the LCM of all given times to work with whole-number rates instead of fractions.

solvedExamples.problem: Pipe A can fill a tank in 6 hours and pipe B can fill it in 4 hours. How long will both pipes take to fill the tank together?
steps: Rate of A = 1/6, Rate of B = 1/4.

solvedExamples.problem: A pipe can fill a tank in 5 hours. Due to a leak, it takes 6 hours to fill. How long would the leak alone take to empty the full tank?
solvedExamples.solution: The leak alone would empty the tank in 30 hours.
steps: Pipe's rate = 1/5, combined rate with leak = 1/6.
Leak alone empties the tank in 30 hours.

solvedExamples.problem: Two pipes A and B can fill a tank in 12 and 15 hours respectively. A third pipe C can empty the full tank in 20 hours. If all three are opened together, how long will it take to fill the tank?
steps: Rate of A = 1/12, Rate of B = 1/15, Rate of C = −1/20.

references.title: Math is Fun — Work Rate Problems
